Name: jacobian_based_mixed_precision_quantization
Title: Dynamic Jacobian-Based Mixed Precision Quantization for Latency-Critical Inference
Short Hypothesis: Can we leverage Jacobian-based layer sensitivity to dynamically adjust mixed precision quantization during inference, thereby optimizing both accuracy and latency in real-time applications?
Related Work: Existing literature primarily focuses on static mixed precision quantization or heuristic models that fail to adaptively respond to changing input distributions. This proposal uniquely integrates Jacobian sensitivity analysis to adaptively optimize precision during inference, addressing limitations of prior works in dynamic environments.
Abstract: We propose a novel dynamic mixed precision quantization framework that utilizes Jacobian-based sensitivity analysis to optimize inference performance in latency-critical applications. By examining the sensitivity of each layer's output with respect to input perturbations, our method dynamically adjusts the precision of weights and activations during inference, providing a more tailored approach compared to static quantization methods. The framework is designed to minimize latency while maintaining accuracy, making it particularly suitable for real-time applications such as mobile devices and edge computing. We will evaluate our method on a variety of deep learning models across multiple tasks, comparing its performance against state-of-the-art static mixed precision methods. The expected outcome is a significant reduction in inference time without compromising accuracy, demonstrating the practical viability of adaptive quantization strategies in production environments.
Experiments: 1. Implement a Jacobian sensitivity analysis module to evaluate layer-wise sensitivity metrics during inference.
2. Develop a dynamic precision adjustment algorithm that utilizes the sensitivity metrics to adjust quantization levels in real-time.
3. Evaluate the proposed method against standard mixed precision quantization techniques on benchmark datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10) using latency and accuracy as metrics.
4. Perform ablation studies to understand the impact of different sensitivity thresholds on performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential challenges include the computational overhead of real-time Jacobian calculations, which may negate latency benefits. Additionally, the method may require careful tuning to avoid excessive quantization artifacts. There is also a risk that dynamic adjustments may not yield significant performance improvements for all model architectures or tasks.
